title: CASA0023 Learning Diary
text:
CASA0023 Learning Diary
This learning diary presents my weekly engagement with remote sensing concepts, practical workflows, and reflections.
crumbs: 1  CASA0023 Learning Diary

title: CASA0023 Learning Diary
text:
Structure
Each chapter corresponds to one week of the module:

Week 2: Portfolio and Quarto

Week 3: Enhancements

Week 4: Urban Monitoring

Week 5: Urban Heat

Week 6: Google Earth Engine

Week 7: Image Classification

Week 8: Advanced Classification


This diary combines practical outputs, critical reflection, and real-world applications of Earth Observation.
crumbs: 1  CASA0023 Learning Diary

title: Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto
text:
Overview
This week focused on the technical setup required for the module portfolio rather than on remote sensing analysis itself. The practical introduced Xaringan for building presentations, Quarto for creating a structured online learning diary, and GitHub Pages for publishing work online.
crumbs: 2  Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto

title: Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto
text:
What I Learned
One of the most important things I learned this week is that this module is not only about analysing remotely sensed data, but also about how that work is organised, presented, and shared. The idea of using a Quarto book means that the learning diary is cumulative and structured, rather than a set of unrelated weekly notes.
The practical also introduced Xaringan as a way of creating presentations using RMarkdown syntax. This was different from using PowerPoint, because content, layout, and formatting are controlled through code and markdown. Although this seemed more technical at first, it also showed how presentation and reproducibility can be combined.
crumbs: 2  Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto

title: Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto
text:
GitHub and Workflow
Another key part of this week was understanding the role of Git and GitHub in the module workflow. The process of adding, committing, and pushing work means that progress is recorded over time, which is important because the assessment requires regular weekly updates. This made me realise that organising files and maintaining a clear project structure is an important part of remote sensing practice, not just a background task.
crumbs: 2  Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto

title: Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto
text:
Reflection
Compared with Week 1, this week felt less focused on image analysis and more focused on infrastructure. At first, setting up Quarto, Xaringan, and GitHub Pages felt technical and slightly overwhelming. However, it became clear that these tools are central to the module because they provide a way to document progress, publish outputs, and connect code with communication.
This week changed how I think about the learning diary. I now understand it not simply as a written reflection, but as part of a digital portfolio that develops over time.
crumbs: 2  Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto

title: Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto
text:
Future Application
The skills introduced this week will be useful throughout the rest of the module. In particular, Quarto will help structure the diary, GitHub Pages will support online publication, and Xaringan provides a way to communicate ideas more dynamically. These tools seem especially useful for future work where analysis, writing, and presentation need to be combined in one workflow.
crumbs: 2  Week 2 – Portfolio, Xaringan and Quarto

title: Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements
text:
Overview
This week focused on atmospheric correction, geometric correction, and image enhancement techniques in remote sensing. The lecture explained how raw satellite data is affected by the atmosphere and how it needs to be processed before analysis.
crumbs: 3  Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements

title: Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements
text:
Atmospheric Correction
I learned that raw satellite images are not purely showing the Earth’s surface. The atmosphere affects the signal through scattering and absorption.
The Dark Object Subtraction (DOS) method assumes that the darkest pixels should be close to zero. By subtracting this value, we can reduce atmospheric effects and estimate surface reflectance.
This helped me understand the difference between Digital Number (DN), radiance, and reflectance.
crumbs: 3  Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements

title: Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements
text:
Image Merging
Satellite images are divided into tiles, meaning one image often cannot cover the whole study area. Therefore, multiple images need to be merged together.
This requires selecting images with:

similar dates
low cloud cover

This made me realise that data preparation is an important step in remote sensing.
crumbs: 3  Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements

title: Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements
text:
Image Enhancement
Several enhancement methods were introduced:

NDVI (vegetation index)
Filtering (smoothing or sharpening)
Texture analysis (GLCM)
PCA (reducing data dimensions)

These methods help highlight different features in the data.
crumbs: 3  Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements

title: Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements
text:
Reflection
This week helped me understand that remote sensing is not just about viewing images, but about processing data. Different methods can change how we interpret the same dataset.
I also realised that choosing the right method depends on the research goal.
crumbs: 3  Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements

title: Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements
text:
Future Application
These techniques will be useful in future work, especially for:

flood mapping
urban analysis
environmental monitoring
crumbs: 3  Week 3 – Corrections and Enhancements

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
text:
Overview
This week explored how remote sensing can support policy and decision-making, particularly in dynamic urban contexts. The focus shifted from long-term environmental analysis to short-term event monitoring using Earth Observation data.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
section: Case Study: Urban Change Monitoring in Tehran
text:
Case Study: Urban Change Monitoring in Tehran
This practical investigates urban change in Tehran using Sentinel-1 SAR data. Instead of focusing on long-term urban expansion, the analysis aims to identify short-term anomalies in the built environment that may relate to urban events, disruptions, or structural changes.
Such monitoring is particularly relevant in contexts where rapid situational awareness is required and ground-based data is limited.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
text:
Remote Sensing Data
Sentinel-1 Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) data was used in this analysis.
SAR data is suitable because:

it operates independently of weather and daylight conditions

it captures structural characteristics of urban surfaces

it enables reliable change detection between time periods
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
text:
Method
Two time periods were selected, and mean SAR images were generated for each period. A difference image was then calculated to detect changes in backscatter.
A threshold was applied to extract areas of significant change, and small isolated pixels were removed to reduce noise. The final output highlights areas of strong SAR change across the city.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
text:
Results



Tehran SAR Change Detection


Figure 1. Sentinel-1 SAR change detection in Tehran, highlighting areas of strong urban surface change between two time periods.
The highlighted areas (yellow) represent locations with significant changes in SAR backscatter. These may indicate structural changes such as building damage, construction activity, or other urban surface disturbances.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
text:
Application to Policy
This approach supports urban policy and decision-making in several ways:

rapid identification of areas experiencing change

support for emergency response and damage assessment

improved situational awareness in dynamic urban environments

Rather than focusing on long-term sustainability planning, this approach is more relevant to crisis monitoring and short-term decision support.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
text:
Link to Policy Frameworks
This work aligns more closely with disaster risk reduction and crisis-response frameworks.
In particular, it supports the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction by enabling rapid assessment of urban change and improving decision-making during emergencies.
It also has relevance for humanitarian applications, where remote sensing provides critical spatial information in data-scarce environments.
While not directly targeting sustainability goals, it may indirectly contribute to SDG 11 by supporting urban resilience and recovery planning.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
text:
Reflection
This week expanded my understanding of remote sensing applications beyond traditional environmental monitoring.
I found it particularly valuable that SAR data can detect structural changes in urban areas, making it useful for dynamic and complex situations.
However, one limitation is that SAR change detection identifies anomalous surface changes but cannot determine the exact cause without additional data or validation.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
text:
Practical Reflection
The practical demonstrated how change detection workflows can be implemented using real satellite data.
It highlighted the importance of data selection, filtering, and thresholding in producing meaningful outputs.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)
text:
Future Application
This approach could be extended by integrating additional datasets, such as optical imagery or higher-resolution data, to improve interpretation.
It has strong potential for applications in disaster response, urban resilience, and real-time monitoring.
crumbs: 4  Week 4 – Remote Sensing and Policy (Urban Event Monitoring)

title: Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat
text:
Overview
This week focused on thermal remote sensing and the measurement of land surface temperature (LST). The lecture introduced how satellite data can be used to analyse temperature patterns in urban environments, particularly in relation to the urban heat island effect.
crumbs: 5  Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat

title: Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat
text:
Key Concepts
A key distinction introduced this week is between land surface temperature (LST) and air temperature.

LST measures the temperature of the Earth’s surface as observed by a satellite sensor

Air temperature is measured near the ground and influenced by atmospheric conditions

Thermal remote sensing allows us to estimate LST using thermal infrared bands, such as those available in Landsat data.
crumbs: 5  Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat

title: Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat
text:
Method
In this practical, Landsat 8/9 Collection 2 Level 2 data was used to derive land surface temperature for Tehran during the summer period.
A cloud mask was applied to remove low-quality pixels, and a median composite was generated. The thermal band (ST_B10) was then converted to degrees Celsius using the official scaling factors provided by USGS.
crumbs: 5  Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat

title: Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat
text:
Results



Tehran LST


Figure 1. Summer land surface temperature (LST) in Tehran derived from Landsat 8/9 data.
The map shows clear spatial variation in temperature across the city. Urban areas appear significantly warmer than surrounding mountainous regions, indicating a strong urban heat island effect.
crumbs: 5  Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat

title: Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat
text:
Application
Thermal remote sensing can support urban policy and planning in several ways:

identifying urban heat hotspots

informing green infrastructure planning

supporting climate adaptation strategies

By providing spatially continuous temperature data, remote sensing enables cities to better understand and respond to heat-related risks.
crumbs: 5  Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat

title: Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat
text:
Reflection
This week expanded my understanding of remote sensing beyond visible imagery to include thermal processes.
I found it particularly interesting that satellite data can be used to estimate surface temperature at a city-wide scale. The distinction between LST and air temperature is also important, as they represent different environmental processes.
crumbs: 5  Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat

title: Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat
text:
Practical Reflection
The practical demonstrated how relatively simple workflows can produce meaningful environmental insights.
Compared to previous weeks, the focus was less on complex processing and more on interpreting results in an urban context.
crumbs: 5  Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat

title: Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat
text:
Limitations
One limitation is that land surface temperature does not directly represent human thermal comfort, which depends on additional factors such as humidity and wind.
In addition, the spatial resolution of Landsat (30 m) may not capture fine-scale urban variations.
crumbs: 5  Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat

title: Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat
text:
Future Application
This approach could be extended by combining LST with vegetation indices (e.g. NDVI) to better understand the relationship between green space and urban temperature.
It also has strong potential for applications in climate adaptation and urban sustainability planning.
crumbs: 5  Week 5 – Temperature and Urban Heat

title: Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis
text:
Overview
This week introduced Google Earth Engine (GEE) as a cloud-based platform for remote sensing analysis. The focus was on learning the basic workflow of loading, filtering, compositing, clipping, and visualising satellite imagery.
crumbs: 6  Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis

title: Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis
text:
Key Concepts
Unlike previous practicals that relied on local processing, GEE allows satellite data to be processed remotely on Google’s servers. This makes it easier to work with large Earth observation datasets efficiently.
The main concepts covered this week included:

loading image collections
filtering by date, cloud cover, and location
creating composite images
clipping data to a study area
applying simple band math such as NDVI
crumbs: 6  Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis

title: Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis
text:
Method
In this practical, Landsat 8 surface reflectance data was used to analyse Tehran.
The workflow involved:

filtering imagery by date and cloud cover
generating a median composite
clipping the imagery to the study area
visualising the RGB composite
calculating NDVI using the near-infrared and red bands
crumbs: 6  Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis

title: Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis
text:
Results



Tehran RGB


Figure 1. Median Landsat RGB composite of Tehran clipped to the study area.
The RGB composite provides a clear overview of the city’s urban structure and surrounding mountainous landscape.



Tehran NDVI


Figure 2. NDVI derived from Landsat data showing vegetation distribution across Tehran.
The NDVI image highlights greener areas within and around the city, making it easier to identify vegetation compared with the RGB image alone.
crumbs: 6  Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis

title: Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis
text:
Application
This workflow demonstrates how GEE can support rapid urban environmental analysis. NDVI is particularly useful for identifying vegetation cover, which can support studies of green space, urban ecology, and environmental planning.
crumbs: 6  Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis

title: Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis
text:
Reflection
This week helped me understand the logic of working with image collections in GEE. Compared with previous workflows, I found GEE efficient for loading and processing large datasets.
It was also useful to see how simple band math can transform a basic satellite image into a more interpretable environmental indicator.
crumbs: 6  Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis

title: Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis
text:
Limitations
Although NDVI is useful for mapping vegetation, it does not capture all aspects of environmental quality. It is also sensitive to the time period selected and the quality of the input imagery.
crumbs: 6  Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis

title: Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis
text:
Future Application
This workflow could be extended to more advanced analysis, including classification, change detection, or linking vegetation patterns to temperature and urban heat.
crumbs: 6  Week 6 – Google Earth Engine and Basic Image Analysis

title: Week 7 – Image Classification
text:
Overview
This week introduced supervised classification using Google Earth Engine (GEE). The aim was to classify land cover types based on training data and spectral information from satellite imagery.
crumbs: 7  Week 7 – Image Classification

title: Week 7 – Image Classification
text:
Key Concepts
Classification is a process of assigning each pixel in an image to a specific land cover class. In supervised classification, training samples are manually defined and used to train a model.
Random Forest was used in this practical, which is an ensemble machine learning method that combines multiple decision trees to improve classification performance.
crumbs: 7  Week 7 – Image Classification

title: Week 7 – Image Classification
text:
Method
Sentinel-2 surface reflectance data was used for classification.
The workflow included:

loading Sentinel-2 imagery

filtering by date, location, and cloud cover

generating a median composite

creating training samples (urban, vegetation, water)

extracting spectral values from the image

training a Random Forest classifier

applying the classifier to the full image
crumbs: 7  Week 7 – Image Classification

title: Week 7 – Image Classification
text:
Results



Classification Map


Figure 1. Land cover classification of Tehran using Random Forest (urban = red, vegetation = green, water = blue).
The classification map distinguishes between urban areas, vegetation, and water. Urban areas dominate the central region, while vegetation is more prominent on the outskirts.
However, a large proportion of areas classified as vegetation appear to correspond to agricultural land (cropland) rather than natural vegetation. This suggests that the classifier is capturing spectral similarities between vegetation types, but is unable to distinguish between different land use categories within the vegetation class.
crumbs: 7  Week 7 – Image Classification

title: Week 7 – Image Classification
text:
Application
Image classification is widely used in urban studies, including land use mapping, environmental monitoring, and change detection.
This method enables large-scale automated mapping of land cover, which can support urban planning and environmental policy.
crumbs: 7  Week 7 – Image Classification

title: Week 7 – Image Classification
text:
Reflection
This week introduced machine learning concepts in remote sensing. I found it particularly useful to understand how training data influences classification results.
The results highlight that classification outputs are highly dependent on how training samples are defined. In this case, the vegetation class likely includes both natural vegetation and cropland, indicating that spectral similarity can limit the ability to distinguish land use types.
This demonstrates the importance of carefully designing training data and class definitions.
crumbs: 7  Week 7 – Image Classification

title: Week 7 – Image Classification
text:
Limitations
The classification accuracy depends heavily on the quality and quantity of training data. Misclassification can occur if classes are not well separated.
Additionally, spectral similarity between different land cover types (e.g. cropland and natural vegetation) can reduce model performance.
crumbs: 7  Week 7 – Image Classification

title: Week 7 – Image Classification
text:
Future Work
Future work could include:

introducing additional classes (e.g. separating cropland and natural vegetation)

increasing the number and quality of training samples

performing accuracy assessment using validation data

comparing different classifiers (e.g. SVM, CART)
crumbs: 7  Week 7 – Image Classification

title: Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis
text:
Overview
This week focused on analysing urban environments using spectral indices and sub-pixel classification techniques in Google Earth Engine.
The aim was to explore how mixed land cover within a single pixel can be better represented, particularly in complex urban landscapes.
crumbs: 8  Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis

title: Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis
text:
Key Concepts
Sub-pixel analysis (spectral unmixing) estimates the proportion of different land cover types within a single pixel.
Unlike traditional classification, which assigns one class per pixel, this method captures mixed surfaces such as urban areas containing vegetation or bare soil.
NDVI was also used to identify vegetation patterns and support interpretation.
crumbs: 8  Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis

title: Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis
text:
Method
Sentinel-2 surface reflectance imagery was used.
The workflow included:

loading Sentinel-2 data
filtering by date, location, and cloud cover
generating a median composite
visualising RGB imagery
calculating NDVI
defining endmembers (urban, vegetation, water, bare earth)
extracting spectral signatures
applying spectral unmixing
constraining fractions to ensure valid outputs
crumbs: 8  Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis

title: Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis
text:
Results

RGB Composite



RGB


Figure 1. True colour RGB composite of Tehran.
The RGB image shows clear urban structure, with dense built-up areas concentrated in the city centre and more heterogeneous land cover at the outskirts.



NDVI



NDVI


Figure 2. NDVI map highlighting vegetation distribution.
Vegetation is mainly located on the outskirts of the city. In this context, many high NDVI areas likely represent cropland rather than natural vegetation.



Sub-pixel Classification (Constrained)



Fractions


Figure 3. Sub-pixel land cover fractions derived using spectral unmixing.
The constrained fraction map shows a clear spatial pattern of land cover distribution. Urban areas dominate the central region of Tehran (red), while vegetation (green) is more prominent in surrounding agricultural areas. Water bodies (blue) are clearly identifiable.
Compared to unconstrained outputs, this result is more realistic, as fractions are forced to sum to one and negative values are avoided.
crumbs: 8  Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis

title: Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis
text:
Application
Sub-pixel analysis is particularly useful in urban environments where land cover is highly mixed.
It allows for:

identifying urban intensity
detecting green infrastructure
supporting urban planning and sustainability analysis

This approach is valuable for understanding urban form beyond simple classification.
crumbs: 8  Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis

title: Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis
text:
Reflection
This week introduced a more advanced approach to classification. I found sub-pixel analysis particularly insightful, as it better represents the complexity of urban environments.
It highlighted how traditional classification may oversimplify land cover, especially in areas with mixed surfaces.
crumbs: 8  Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis

title: Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis
text:
Limitations
The results depend heavily on the selection of endmembers. If training areas are not representative, the fractions may be biased.
Additionally, spectral similarity between urban surfaces and bare land can lead to overestimation of urban fractions, especially in arid environments like Tehran.
crumbs: 8  Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis

title: Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis
text:
Future Work
Future improvements could include:

using additional spectral indices to improve separation
incorporating higher-resolution data
testing alternative unmixing approaches
validating results with ground truth data
crumbs: 8  Week 8 – Temperature and Sub-pixel Analysis

title: Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing
text:
Overview
This week introduced the basic foundations of remote sensing through both the lecture and the practical. The lecture focused on what remote sensing is, how data are collected, the main observation platforms, key satellite data sources, and some common applications. The practical extended this by introducing a basic workflow for working with remotely sensed data, including downloading Sentinel and Landsat imagery, exploring image products in QGIS and SNAP, and beginning simple raster processing and comparison.
crumbs: 2  Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing

title: Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing
text:
Key Learning
One of the most important insights from this week is that remote sensing is more than simply viewing satellite imagery. It is a structured process involving sensors, platforms, data products, resolution, and analytical workflows. Even at an introductory level, decisions such as study area selection, cloud cover thresholds, and data source choice significantly influence the outputs.
crumbs: 2  Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing

title: Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing
text:
Practical Insights
The practical session helped translate theory into application. By working with Sentinel and Landsat data, I began to understand that different sensors have different strengths and limitations. Sentinel offers higher spatial detail, while Landsat provides a longer temporal archive, which is valuable for historical analysis.
I also compared different software environments:

QGIS – intuitive for visualisation and basic processing

SNAP – specialised for remote sensing workflows

R – suitable for statistical analysis and reproducibility

This highlighted how software choice can shape analytical approaches.
crumbs: 2  Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing

title: Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing
text:
Methods Introduced
This week introduced early-stage workflow steps, including:

data sourcing (Sentinel, Landsat)

filtering by cloud cover and date

basic raster visualisation

introduction to spectral signatures

simple statistical comparisons
crumbs: 2  Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing

title: Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing
text:
Reflection
A key takeaway is that remote sensing moves beyond visual interpretation towards quantitative analysis. Even at this stage, the idea of distinguishing land cover types (e.g. vegetation, water, built-up areas) using spectral information became clear.
The introduction to GitHub and .gitignore also highlighted the importance of workflow management when dealing with large datasets. This is particularly relevant for maintaining a structured and reproducible learning diary.
crumbs: 2  Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing

title: Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing
text:
Conclusion
Overall, Week 1 provided a structured foundation for remote sensing. It reframed my understanding from simply observing satellite imagery to engaging with a full analytical workflow involving data selection, processing, software environments, and interpretation.
crumbs: 2  Week 1 – Getting Started with Remote Sensing
